1: A revelation of God: Solomon's love (ʾāhaḇ, H157) for many foreign women stands against the covenant love YHWH has commanded — "you shall love the LORD your God with all your heart" (Deuteronomy 6:5). The king who was supposed to embody Israel's covenant fidelity is defined by his love for those whom the Torah warned against. The failure of the human king at the point of his heart's loyalty — loving what he should not love — is the theological problem that only a king whose heart is constitutively aligned with the Father can solve. "He who did not know sin became sin for us" (2 Corinthians 5:21): Christ succeeds precisely at the point where Solomon catastrophically fails.
2: A revelation of God: "YHWH had told the Israelites, 'You must not intermarry with them, nor they with you, for they will surely turn your hearts after their gods.'" The warning was specific, public, and covenantally grounded. Solomon's disobedience is not a mistake born of ignorance but a choice against known divine instruction. This reveals God's covenant seriousness: the warnings attached to covenant relationship are not rhetorical but predictive. The NT carries the principle forward: "Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" (2 Corinthians 6:14) and "the love of the world is enmity with God" (James 4:4). Solomon's story is the canonical illustration of what these warnings prevent.
3: A revelation of God: 700 wives of royal standing and 300 concubines — the explicit violation of Deuteronomy 17:17 ("he must not multiply wives for himself, lest his heart turn away"). The Torah's law for kings was written for this precise scenario; Solomon's accumulation of women mirrors his accumulation of gold and horses as a three-fold violation of the Deuteronomic royal ideal (Deuteronomy 17:14-20). The correlation between the multiplication of wives and the turning of the heart (wayyaṭṭû nāšāyw ʾet libbô) is explicit in the text. The heart — the locus of covenant loyalty — is turned by the accumulation that the Torah forbade. Christ's covenant fidelity is the counter-pattern: his heart is never divided, his loyalty never purchased or seduced.
4: A revelation of God: "As Solomon grew old, his wives turned his heart after other gods, and his heart was not fully devoted (šālēm, whole/complete) to the LORD his God, as the heart of his father David had been." The lēḇāḇ šālēm — the whole, undivided heart — is the Deuteronomic standard for covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 6:5; 1 Kings 8:61). Solomon's heart is found deficient at old age, after all his wisdom, after the temple dedication, after two divine appearances. The failure reveals that wisdom, worship, and divine encounter do not guarantee heart-wholeness. Only the one who is the divine Word incarnate — whose will is perfectly conformed to the Father's at every point (John 6:38; Hebrews 10:7) — can offer the lēḇāḇ šālēm that the covenant requires.
5: A revelation of God: Solomon follows Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians and Milcom the detestable god of the Ammonites. The wisdom king whose temple celebrated YHWH's unique glory (1 Kings 8:23, "there is no God like you in heaven above or on earth beneath") now pays homage to the gods of Israel's neighbors. The trajectory from temple-builder to idol-follower reveals the tragic insufficiency of even the greatest human achievement in honoring God. No temple built with human hands and no wisdom derived from human intellect guarantees ongoing fidelity. The NT response is the indwelling Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16-17; 1 Kings 6:19): the only temple that will not be defiled because its architect and inhabitant is God himself.
6: A revelation of God: "Solomon did evil in the LORD's sight and did not follow the LORD completely (millēʾ ʾaḥᵃrê, did not fill up / go fully after) as his father David had done." The narrator's verdict uses covenantal language: following YHWH completely (millēʾ ʾaḥᵃrê) is the OT's expression for wholehearted covenant fidelity. David, for all his sins, is the standard; Solomon falls short of even the David-standard. The NT's standard is higher still — "Be perfect, as your heavenly Father is perfect" (Matthew 5:48) — and is only achievable through Christ's imputed righteousness and the Spirit's transformative work.
7: A revelation of God: Solomon builds a high place for Chemosh (Moab) and for Molech (Ammon) on the hill east of Jerusalem — the Mount of Olives, directly facing the temple. The placement is theologically devastating: the site of idolatrous sacrifice (Molech worship involved child sacrifice) is set in direct visual confrontation with the house of YHWH. The Mount of Olives will later become the site of Christ's prayer in Gethsemane (Luke 22:39) and his ascension (Acts 1:12) — a redemptive reoccupation of the same hill. The NT's repeated use of the Mount of Olives in the passion narrative may carry an implicit echo of this reversal: where Solomon established Gentile abominations, Christ offers his perfect obedience to the Father.
8: A revelation of God: "He did the same for all his foreign wives, who burned incense and offered sacrifices to their gods." The temple system, designed to be the exclusive locus of Israel's worship, is now surrounded by competing shrines all within Solomon's purview. The compromise is total. What the temple was built to embody — the exclusive claim of YHWH on his people's worship — is undone by the one who built it. The lesson the NT draws: no human institution, however divinely ordered, automatically guarantees the holiness of its builders. Only the living presence of the Spirit maintains the purity of the temple that is Christ's body (1 Corinthians 3:16-17).
9: A revelation of God: "The LORD was angry (wayyitʾanap, H599 — a strong term for divine anger) with Solomon because his heart had turned (nāṭāh libbô) from YHWH the God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice." The divine anger is presented as a covenant response to a specific, aggravated covenant violation: YHWH had appeared to Solomon personally (1 Kings 3 and 1 Kings 9) — greater revelation entails greater accountability. The NT carries this principle: "For everyone to whom much was given, much will be required" (Luke 12:48); and Hebrews 10:26-29 warns that deliberate sin after receiving the knowledge of the truth incurs severer judgment. Solomon's privileged access to divine revelation makes his apostasy the more culpable.
10: A revelation of God: "He had warned him expressly about this very thing — not to follow other gods. But Solomon did not keep the LORD's command." The word ṣiwwāh (commanded, H6680) is the standard verb for covenant obligation — what YHWH commands, Israel is bound to obey. Solomon's failure is not ignorance but disobedience against explicit divine command. The NT's characterization of sin as lawlessness (anomia, 1 John 3:4) — violation of known divine standard — is grounded in narratives exactly like this one. Christ's perfect obedience (hupakoē, Romans 5:19) is the reversal of Solomon's disobedience: "as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous."
11: A revelation of God and a shadow: "I will certainly tear the kingdom from you and give it to one of your servants." The covenant consequence is judicial and declarative: YHWH is the one who gives and takes kingship. The term qāraʿ (tear, H7167) — the same word used in v30 for Ahijah's tearing of the garment — will become the controlling image of the chapter. The transfer of the kingdom from Solomon's house is not a defeat of YHWH's purposes but a demonstration of them: the Davidic covenant's persistence through and beyond the Solomonic failure is itself the proof of God's sovereign faithfulness. The NT reveals that God uses human failure as the occasion of redemptive advance: the division of the kingdom leads ultimately to the exile that produces the diaspora through which the gospel spreads (Acts 8:1).
12: A revelation of God: "For the sake of your father David I will not do this during your lifetime. I will tear it away from your son." The Davidic covenant creates a reserve of grace that persists through Solomon's failure: judgment is deferred, not cancelled. This pattern of deferred-but-certain judgment out of covenant loyalty to prior faithfulness is consistent throughout Kings (see also 1 Kings 15:4; 2 Kings 8:19). The NT's ultimate expression of this pattern is the cross itself: the judgment that human sin deserved was not cancelled but absorbed — into Christ — so that mercy could be extended. God's deference toward Solomon points to God's ultimate deferral of judgment through the substitution of his Son.
13: A revelation of God: "Yet I will not tear away the entire kingdom. I will give one tribe to your son, for the sake of my servant David and for the sake of Jerusalem, which I have chosen." The preservation of one tribe is YHWH's covenant minimum — enough to keep the Davidic line alive and the Jerusalem temple standing. This is not divine minimalism but divine faithfulness operating at the point of maximum human failure. The phrase "for the sake of my servant David" (bᵃʿăḇûr dāwîḏ ʿaḇdî) becomes the formula for grace that survives moral collapse. The NT's "for the sake of Christ" (dia Christon, Ephesians 4:32; Colossians 3:13) carries the identical structure: what the covenant person achieved by faithfulness becomes the ground on which others receive mercy they have not earned.
14: A revelation of God: "The LORD raised up (wayyāqem YHWH) an adversary (śāṭān) against Solomon: Hadad the Edomite." The word śāṭān (H7854) — "adversary" — is used here in its original, non-demonic sense: an enemy raised up by God as a covenant instrument of discipline. The same word describes the accuser in Job 1-2. YHWH's sovereignty over adversarial forces — his ability to raise them against the disobedient and to use them for covenant purposes — is the OT foundation for the NT's doctrine that even demonic opposition is bounded by divine permission (Job 1:12; Luke 22:31-32; 1 Corinthians 10:13). Christ confronts and defeats the ultimate śāṭān (Matthew 4:10; Revelation 20:10).
15: A shadow: when David had been in Edom, Joab had struck down every male in Edom (cf. Psalms 60:1-2, associated with this campaign). David's military legacy creates the adversary Solomon will face. The consequences of past actions — even justified covenant warfare — ripple forward into subsequent generations. This is the OT's patient historical vision: causes have effects across time. The NT's eschatological patience ("the Lord is not slow... but patient toward you," 2 Peter 3:9) and the doctrine of the sins of the fathers (Exodus 20:5) both assume this same long temporal arc that culminates in Christ's final reckoning of all things (Acts 17:31).
16: A shadow: Joab and all Israel remained six months in Edom until every male was killed — a total military campaign. The completeness of David's suppression of Edom created the conditions for Hadad's survival as refugee and eventual adversary. History's "solutions" that eliminate one problem often create future ones. Only the eschatological judgment of Christ provides the final, non-cyclical resolution: "He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death" (1 Corinthians 15:25-26).
17: A shadow: Hadad escapes as a small boy to Egypt — the same Egypt that sheltered Joseph (Gen 39-47) and received the infant Jesus (Matthew 2:14-15). Egypt functions in biblical narrative as a recurring place of refuge from those who would kill God's appointed (Joseph, Moses, the infant Christ). The pattern is not coincidental: Matthew's "out of Egypt I called my son" (Matthew 2:15, citing Hosea 11:1) explicitly maps the Jesus-to-Egypt-and-back journey onto Israel's founding narrative, and this same shadow falls over Hadad's escape.
18: A shadow: Hadad's journey — Midian, Paran, Egypt — echoes the wilderness geography of Israel's own history. Pharaoh's reception of Hadad (giving him a house, land, and sustenance) parallels the Egyptian reception of Jacob's family (Genesis 45:18; 1 Kings 47:11). The recurring pattern of YHWH's covenant purposes advancing through unexpected geographical sojourns and foreign hospitality will reach its apex in the holy family's flight to and return from Egypt (Matthew 2:13-21).
19: A shadow: Hadad wins such favor with Pharaoh that he receives the sister of the queen as wife. The depth of Hadad's Egyptian integration — royal household, wife from the court — establishes him as a credible adversary capable of challenging Solomon. The narrative's quiet preservation of this Edomite thread through the Solomonic era is an instance of the OT's patient story of how YHWH's covenant purposes advance even through the adversaries he raises up. The NT counterpart: even the enemies of the gospel serve its advance (Philippians 1:12).
20: A shadow: Genubath, Hadad's son, grows up among Pharaoh's own sons — an Edomite in the Egyptian court. The child of Israel's adversary raised in Israel's former slave-house, poised to become a future threat, is a micro-narrative of the complex entanglement of nations that God uses for his own purposes. The wisdom literature's question ("Who can know the thoughts of God?" Isaiah 40:13; Romans 11:34) is embedded in these narratives of extraordinary circumstance: all of this — Hadad's survival, Egyptian marriage, son in Pharaoh's court — is YHWH's preparation of a covenant instrument.
21: A shadow: when Hadad hears that David and Joab are dead, he asks to return home. The deaths of his enemies free him to pursue his purpose. The interaction between personal opportunity and divine orchestration is characteristic of OT narrative: Hadad acts from self-interest, but YHWH has raised him up (v14) for a larger purpose. The NT's providence theology (Romans 8:28, "all things work together for good") is not the absence of human motivation but God's sovereign direction of genuinely motivated human actions toward his purposes.
22: A shadow: Hadad's "nothing — but please, let me go." The adversary's determination to return cannot be deterred by comfort; the covenant purpose that raised him up (v14) is stronger than Egyptian prosperity. The certainty of God's instruments fulfilling their purpose runs throughout biblical narrative — Jonah cannot outrun his mission (Jonah 1:3-17), Paul cannot remain in Damascus once called to the Gentiles (Acts 9:15-25), and the adversaries of God's people accomplish the discipline YHWH intends (Habakkuk 1:6). Only Christ himself has full sovereignty over when and how divine purposes are accomplished.
23: A revelation of God: "God also raised up (wayyāqem ʾĕlōhîm) another adversary against Solomon: Rezon son of Eliada." The second adversary is introduced with the same sovereign verb: YHWH raises up adversaries as instruments of covenant discipline. The repetition (Hadad in v14, Rezon here) emphasizes the pattern: disobedience multiplies adversaries; the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 are beginning their systematic fulfillment. The NT's doctrine of divine discipline — "For the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives" (Hebrews 12:6, citing Proverbs 3:11-12) — has this long OT history of God-raised adversaries as its background.
24: A shadow: Rezon gathers men around him after David destroys Hadadezer's forces, goes to Damascus, and becomes king there. The collapse of one Aramean power structure (Hadadezer's) creates the opening for a new, adversarial Aramean state (Rezon's Damascus). The pattern recurs throughout OT history: destroyed threats generate successor threats. This is not divine failure but covenant pedagogy — the instability of the present age will only end when the one who "disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him" (Colossians 2:15) brings the final peace.
25: A shadow: Rezon is a persistent adversary throughout Solomon's lifetime — combined with Hadad, the two threats bracket the Solomonic era. The peaceful interlude of Solomon's early reign (1 Kings 4:24-25, "Solomon had peace on all sides") is over. The fragility of earthly peace — dependent on the faithfulness of the human king who maintains it — contrasts with the peace that Christ gives: "not as the world gives" (John 14:27), grounded not in military balance but in the reconciliation of humanity to God (Romans 5:1; Ephesians 2:14-17).
26: A shadow: Jeroboam son of Nebat, an Ephraimite and Solomon's own official, also rebels. The third adversary is internal — not an Edomite or Aramean outsider but a capable Israelite insider whom Solomon himself had promoted. The structure of this chapter is deliberately comprehensive: external enemies (Hadad, Rezon) and internal betrayal (Jeroboam) simultaneously close in on the Solomonic kingdom. The Davidic ideal requires a king whose kingdom is at peace from without and whose servants are loyal within. Only the messianic king of Ps 72 and Isa 11 fulfills both conditions permanently.
27: A shadow: the background to Jeroboam's rebellion is Solomon's building program — the Millo and the repair of David's city wall. The construction that secured the capital also generated the resentment (forced labor, cf. 1 Kings 5) that produced the rebel. Solomon's greatest achievement (the city) carries the seeds of its own undoing. The NT's paradox is the inverse: Christ's apparent defeat (the cross) is the instrument of ultimate victory (resurrection). What appears to destroy the Davidic project in fact establishes it permanently (Acts 2:23-24).
28: A shadow: Jeroboam is a capable (gibbôr ḥayil, a man of valor/ability) young man whom Solomon himself places over the forced labor of the house of Joseph. Solomon creates his own replacement — the king raises up the instrument of his own judgment. This irony is a recurring biblical pattern: God often uses the gifts and initiative of the very people whom the powerful have elevated and exploited. Joseph, raised to power by Pharaoh, becomes Israel's deliverer; David, promoted by Saul, replaces him. The NT pattern: Jesus is "elevated" on the cross by those who oppose him, and thereby defeats them (Colossians 2:13-15).
29: A revelation of God: Ahijah the Shilonite meets Jeroboam on the road and performs a prophetic sign-act — wearing a new garment. The choice of a new garment is significant: the newness (ḥādāš) of the cloak mirrors the newness of what is about to be announced — a new dynastic arrangement. Prophetic sign-acts in the OT are not mere illustrations; they are performative declarations that set in motion what they signify (cf. Jeremiah 19:10-11; Ezek 4). The tearing of the garment that follows is a enacted word that will not return void (cf. Isaiah 55:11). The NT's ultimate "new thing" (kainós) is the new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31; Luke 22:20) established by the one who makes "all things new" (Revelation 21:5).
30: A revelation of God: Ahijah tears the new garment into twelve pieces. The violence of the act — a new garment torn — makes visible what disobedience does to the covenant unity of Israel. The twelve pieces correspond to the twelve tribes, now about to be redistributed. Sign-acts in the prophetic tradition enact the divine word at the level of the body and the material: the torn garment is what YHWH will do to the kingdom. The NT's enacted word is the broken body of Christ — "this is my body, broken for you" (1 Corinthians 11:24) — which, unlike the torn kingdom, becomes the instrument of unity rather than division: "one body, one Spirit" (Ephesians 4:4).
31: A direct prophecy and type: "Take ten pieces for yourself. For this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I am tearing the kingdom from Solomon's hand and giving you ten tribes." The tearing of the kingdom is YHWH's direct action (ʾănî qōrēaʿ, I am tearing), with Ahijah's sign-act as its enactment. Jeroboam's commission parallels David's original anointing (1 Samuel 16:13) and prefigures Christ's reception of all authority: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matthew 28:18). As Jeroboam receives ten tribes from the one who held them, so the risen Christ receives all nations from the Father (Psalms 2:8).
32: A revelation of God: "He will keep one tribe — for the sake of my servant David and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen from all the tribes of Israel." The divine election of both person (David) and place (Jerusalem) is the covenant's double anchor. Neither collapses under the weight of Solomon's failure. The perseverance of both through the division narrative is the OT's demonstration that divine election does not depend on human performance. The NT's "in Christ" language carries the same structure: "he chose us in him before the foundation of the world" (Ephesians 1:4) — election grounded in the Elect One, not in the elect people's merit.
33: A revelation of God: "Because they have abandoned me and worshipped Ashtoreth... Chemosh... Milcom. They have not walked in my ways or done what is right in my eyes, nor kept my statutes and ordinances as David his father did." The covenant indictment is comprehensive: the verbs are communal (they, plural) — Solomon's apostasy is not purely individual but national in its effect. The king's heart is the nation's covenant representative. This is the OT's basis for the NT's federal theology: one representative's failure (Adam) implicates many; one representative's faithfulness (Christ) justifies many (Romans 5:12-19). The Davidic standard (v33b) set for Israel's kings is the shadow of the standard Christ alone meets on behalf of all.
34: A revelation of God: "But I will not take the whole kingdom from him. I will keep him as ruler for the rest of his life, for the sake of my servant David whom I chose, because David kept my commandments and statutes." David's covenant-keeping provides the covering that extends grace to the disobedient Solomon. The substitutionary structure is explicit: David's merits are the ground of Solomon's continuation in office. The NT's explicit claim is the same logic extended infinitely: Christ's perfect covenant-keeping provides the covering for all who are united to him by faith (2 Corinthians 5:21; Philippians 3:9). Solomon benefits from David's righteousness covenantally; the believer benefits from Christ's righteousness infinitely.
35: A revelation of God: "I will take the kingdom from his son's hand and give it to you — ten tribes." The transfer is surgical: one son loses ten-twelfths of his inheritance, not because of his personal sin (he has not yet sinned) but because of his father's. The inter-generational nature of covenant consequences (Exodus 20:5) is uncomfortable but honest: communities and dynasties bear the weight of their leaders' choices. The NT's resolution is the one who bears the weight of all the covenant failures of all the dynasties in his own body, so that those united to him receive the full inheritance rather than a fraction (Colossians 1:12; Hebrews 9:15).
36: A direct promise: "I will give his son one tribe, so that my servant David may always have a nîr (lamp/dominion, H5216) before me in Jerusalem, the city where I have chosen to put my name." The nîr lᵉdāwîḏ — a lamp for David — is a covenantal formula for the perpetuation of the Davidic dynasty (cf. 1 Kings 15:4; 2 Kings 8:19; Psalms 132:17: "There I will make a horn sprout for David; I have prepared a lamp for my anointed"). In Luke 1:69 Zechariah declares that God "has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David" — this is the lamp promise fulfilled. Christ is the promised nîr lᵉdāwîḏ: the light that the Davidic covenant preserved through every crisis until it blazed fully in the one who says "I am the light of the world" (John 8:12).
37: A shadow: "I will take you, and you will reign over all that your heart desires, and you will be king over Israel." Jeroboam's commission follows the pattern of David's: a capable man from an unexpected tribe elevated by divine choice to kingship. The parallel is deliberate and sobering — Jeroboam is given the same opportunity David had, and will fail where David succeeded. The king who reigns "over all that his heart desires" is ultimately the one whose heart desires perfectly what God desires. Psalms 40:8 (cited in Hebrews 10:7 as Christological): "I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart." Only Christ's heart-desire is fully aligned with the Father's; only his reign is therefore unending.
38: A shadow: "If you obey all I command you, walk in my ways, and do what is right in my sight... then I will be with you. I will build you a lasting dynasty as I did for David, and I will give you Israel." The conditional structure — if you obey, then I will establish — is the standard Deuteronomic covenant formula. Jeroboam will fail this condition immediately (1 Kings 12:28-30: golden calves). The permanent dynasty promised conditionally to Jeroboam is granted unconditionally to Christ: "Of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end, on the throne of David and over his kingdom, to establish it and to uphold it" (Isaiah 9:7). The conditional promise points toward the one who meets the condition perfectly and therefore receives the unconditional fulfillment.
39: A shadow: "I will humble (ʾᵃʿanneh, H6031) the descendants of David because of this — but not forever (wᵉlōʾ kol-hayyāmîm)." The "not forever" is one of the OT's most quietly significant phrases. The humiliation of the Davidic line is real — through the divided kingdom, the Assyrian threat, the Babylonian exile, the return as Persian subjects — but it is bounded. The eschatological reservation ("not forever") points beyond every historical humiliation to the restoration YHWH will accomplish. Paul applies the same logic to Israel's current partial hardening: "a partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in. And in this way all Israel will be saved" (Romans 11:25-26). The "not forever" of 1 Kings 11:39 is the OT root of the "until" of Romans 11:25.
40: A shadow: Solomon tries to kill Jeroboam, who flees to Egypt. The anointed king trying to destroy the one whom the prophet has designated is the Solomonic repetition of Saul's pursuit of David (1 Sam 19-26), which is itself repeated in Herod's slaughter of the innocents (Matthew 2:13-16). The pattern of established power attempting to destroy the divinely chosen successor is a recurrent OT structure that reaches its apex in the crucifixion of Christ — "the rulers of the world... would not have crucified the Lord of glory" (1 Corinthians 2:8), yet in doing so they accomplished the very purpose God had ordained (Acts 2:23; 1 Kings 4:27-28).
41: A shadow: "As for the rest of Solomon's acts — all he did and his wisdom — are they not written in the Book of the Acts of Solomon?" The narrator's reference to a now-lost source is an acknowledgment that the canonical text does not capture everything. The wisdom and acts of Solomon that exceeded what is recorded stand as a reminder that even the fullest human achievement is only partially preserved. The NT's equivalent: "Now Jesus did many other signs in the presence of the disciples, which are not written in this book" (John 20:30); "the world itself could not contain the books that would be written" (John 21:25). Solomon's unrecorded acts are finite; Christ's unrecorded works are inexhaustible.
42: A shadow: "Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for forty years." The forty years mirrors other significant OT forty-year periods: the wilderness wandering (Deuteronomy 8:2-4), David's reign (2 Samuel 5:4), the oppression periods in Judges. The number carries a sense of completeness-in-testing. Solomon's forty-year reign is complete in itself and complete in its typological function — the greatest human king has reigned; he has failed; the stage is set for the one who will reign forever. The "forty days" of Jesus's wilderness testing (Matthew 4:1-11) deliberately echoes Israel's forty years; Jesus succeeds where Israel (and Solomon) failed.
43: A shadow: "Then Solomon rested with his ancestors and was buried in the city of his father David. And his son Rehoboam succeeded him as king." The formula "rested with his ancestors" is the standard burial notice for Israel's kings — they sleep, awaiting the resurrection. The Davidic succession continues despite Solomon's failure (Rehoboam, not Jeroboam, inherits Jerusalem and the one tribe), because YHWH's covenant with David does not collapse with Solomon's sin. The "rested with ancestors" formula points forward to the one who also "rested" in death but did not remain — whose resurrection broke the pattern that every previous Davidic king had established (Acts 2:29-32: "David both died and was buried... he foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ, that he was not abandoned to Hades, nor did his flesh see corruption").
